options: After the first capture, the battalion was ordered to withdraw because neighbouring formations had not secured their objectives, leaving it isolated. It was later ordered to recapture Dograi.
The first attack was only a diversionary operation.
Flooding forced the battalion to abandon its positions.

quiz.question: Which gallantry award did Lieutenant Colonel Desmond Hayde receive for his leadership during the Battle of Dograi?

lessons.value: The first Sabre Slayer of 1965—proving that superior skill, courage, and determination could overcome a formidable opponent.
lessons.hook: Flying the tiny Folland Gnat, Trevor Keelor challenged Pakistan's celebrated F-86 Sabre and emerged victorious. His historic victory became one of the defining moments of the 1965 air war and helped earn the Gnat its legendary reputation as the 'Sabre Slayer.'

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Trevor Keelor was born on 24 March 1932 in Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, into an Anglo-Indian family that valued discipline, service, and education. Commissioned into the Indian Air Force during a period of rapid modernisation, he became part of a new generation of fighter pilots entrusted with defending India's skies as jet aviation transformed aerial warfare. The Indian Air Force was inducting aircraft such as the Hawker Hunter, Dassault Mystère IVA, MiG-21, and the compact but highly manoeuvrable Folland Gnat, which would soon become famous during the 1965 Indo-Pakistani War.

On 7 September 1965, Squadron Leader Trevor Keelor, flying a Folland Gnat of No. 23 Squadron, intercepted Pakistani Air Force F-86 Sabres over the Chhamb sector in Jammu and Kashmir. The F-86 Sabre enjoyed an international reputation as one of the finest fighter aircraft of its era, but Keelor used the Gnat's exceptional agility, small size, and skilful positioning to gain the advantage in a close-range dogfight. He shot down one Sabre, becoming the first Indian Air Force pilot to destroy a Pakistani aircraft during the 1965 war. The victory significantly boosted morale within the Indian Air Force and demonstrated that disciplined flying, sound tactics, and courage could overcome even a highly respected adversary.

Trevor's younger brother, Flight Lieutenant Denzil Keelor, also shot down a Pakistani F-86 Sabre only days later, making the Keelor brothers one of the rare sibling pairs in military aviation history to each achieve an aerial victory in the same conflict. Trevor Keelor continued serving with distinction in the Indian Air Force and eventually retired as a Group Captain. Although he retired at a higher rank, he is widely remembered in military history as Wing Commander Trevor Keelor because of his distinguished wartime service. He remained closely associated with the legacy of the 1965 war until his passing on 27 September 2022 at the age of 90.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body:
The air war of 1965 was fiercely contested, with both the Indian Air Force and the Pakistan Air Force striving to gain an operational advantage over the battlefield. Trevor Keelor's victory was historically significant because it was the Indian Air Force's first confirmed aerial victory of the conflict. It also challenged the widespread belief that the F-86 Sabre was unbeatable in air combat. Together with later victories by other Gnat pilots, Keelor's success helped establish the aircraft's famous reputation as the 'Sabre Slayer.'

Although neither side achieved complete control of the skies, the Indian Air Force successfully denied Pakistan unrestricted freedom of air operations over key sectors throughout the war. Keelor's courage, professionalism, and flying skill inspired fellow pilots and strengthened confidence in the Indian Air Force during one of its most important early jet-age conflicts. For his gallantry and outstanding skill in aerial combat, he was awarded the Vir Chakra.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body:
Wing Commander Trevor Keelor occupies a special place in the history of the Indian Air Force as one of the pioneering fighter heroes of the 1965 Indo-Pakistani War. His victory on 7 September 1965 marked a defining moment in the confidence of the Indian Air Force and remains one of the landmark achievements of India's early jet-age combat history.

Along with his brother Denzil Keelor, Trevor symbolised the professionalism, dedication, and inclusive traditions of the post-Independence Indian Air Force. Their achievements demonstrated that excellence in military service transcends community and background, reflecting the merit-based ethos of India's armed forces.

Trevor Keelor lived long enough to witness the remarkable transformation of the Indian Air Force into one of the world's leading modern air forces. His calmness under pressure, exceptional flying skill, and devotion to duty continue to inspire generations of fighter pilots. His story reminds us that in aerial combat, victory depends not only on technology but also on training, judgement, discipline, and the courage of the pilot.

lessons.reflection: Trevor Keelor faced an opponent whose aircraft enjoyed a formidable reputation, yet careful preparation, superior flying skill, and calm decision-making enabled him to prevail. Have you ever succeeded against difficult odds because you relied on preparation and confidence rather than superior resources? What does his story teach us about the importance of mastering our skills before facing a challenge?

Why are Trevor and Denzil Keelor remembered together in Indian Air Force history?
options: They both commanded the same fighter squadron.

They jointly planned India's air operations during the war.

lessons.value: Fearless leadership and unwavering determination in one of the fiercest infantry battles of the 1965 war.
lessons.hook: Even after being hit by multiple bullets, Major Asa Ram Tyagi refused to stop. Leading from the front at Dograi, he inspired his men to capture one of Pakistan's strongest defensive positions, proving that true leadership means never asking others to face dangers you are unwilling to face yourself.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Major Asa Ram Tyagi was born on 2 May 1936 in Fatehpur village of Meerut district (present-day Uttar Pradesh). Raised in a family that valued discipline, patriotism, and service, he developed a strong sense of duty from an early age. After completing his education, he joined the Indian Army and was commissioned into the JAT Regiment, where his courage, physical endurance, and concern for his soldiers quickly earned him respect. By the time of the 1965 Indo-Pakistani War, he was serving as a Company Commander in the 3rd Battalion, JAT Regiment (3 JAT).

In September 1965, India launched an offensive towards Lahore to relieve pressure on the Jammu & Kashmir front. One of the most critical objectives was the capture of Dograi, a heavily fortified Pakistani position beyond the Bambawali–Ravi–Bedian (BRB), or Ichhogil, Canal. During the renewed assault on the night of 21–22 September, Major Tyagi led his company at the forefront of the attack through intense machine-gun, mortar, artillery, and tank fire.

As his troops encountered strongly defended enemy positions supported by Pakistani armour, Major Tyagi personally led assaults to neutralise resistance. Displaying exceptional courage, he attacked enemy tanks with grenades at close range, successfully disabling two of them while encouraging his men to continue the advance. During the fierce fighting he was struck by multiple bullets, but despite his serious injuries he refused evacuation and continued directing his company until the objective had been secured. He eventually collapsed after sustaining five bullet wounds and later succumbed to his injuries. His fearless leadership played a vital role in the successful capture of Dograi, one of the defining infantry victories of the 1965 war.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body:
The capture of Dograi demonstrated the determination, discipline, and offensive capability of the Indian Army during the 1965 Indo-Pakistani War. By leading from the front despite overwhelming enemy fire, Major Asa Ram Tyagi inspired his soldiers to overcome one of Pakistan's strongest forward defensive positions. His courage contributed significantly to the success of 3 JAT in securing Dograi and helped establish the battle as one of the finest examples of infantry combat in independent India's military history.

His actions also reinforced an enduring military principle—that leadership is earned through personal example. By sharing every danger faced by his men, he strengthened their confidence and determination at a critical moment in the battle. For his conspicuous gallantry, inspiring leadership, and supreme sacrifice, Major Asa Ram Tyagi was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body:
Major Asa Ram Tyagi is remembered as one of the outstanding heroes of the Battle of Dograi. Within the JAT Regiment, his courage and devotion to duty continue to inspire officers and soldiers who study the battle as a classic example of determined infantry leadership.

His sacrifice is commemorated through memorials, educational institutions, roads, and remembrance ceremonies held by veterans and the Indian Army. Every year, the JAT Regiment observes Dograi Day to honour the courage of the soldiers who fought in the battle, including Major Tyagi.

His story teaches that great leaders are recognised not only by the orders they give, but by the example they set under the most difficult circumstances. Even today, his life remains a powerful reminder that courage, selflessness, and responsibility towards one's team are timeless qualities that inspire generations.

lessons.reflection: Major Asa Ram Tyagi continued leading his soldiers despite suffering serious injuries because he believed completing the mission and protecting his men were more important than his own safety. Why do you think people are inspired by leaders who are willing to share the same risks as those they lead? How can we demonstrate similar responsibility and courage in our own everyday lives?

options: By attacking them with grenades at close range

modules.title: Liberation and Victory — The 1971 War
modules.focus: India's most decisive military victory — a profound 13-day conflict that created Bangladesh, liberated millions from oppression, and drew acts of extraordinary valour from every branch of the armed forces.
modules.activity: Read the Pakistani Instrument of Surrender signed on 16 December 1971. Write down three aspects of it that surprised or moved you — consider the language used, the scale of the surrender, and what it meant for the people of Bangladesh.
modules.challenge: Research Operation Trident — the Indian Navy's audacious missile-boat strike on Karachi harbour on the night of 4–5 December 1971. Write one paragraph on what it reveals about the power of coordinated joint military operations and how it changed the course of the naval campaign.
modules.summary:
The genesis of the 1971 conflict lay in the brutal suppression of the Bengali independence movement by Pakistan's military in East Pakistan. On the night of 25 March 1971, Operation Searchlight was unleashed — a campaign of organised terror against the civilian population that triggered a humanitarian catastrophe, forcing nearly 10 million refugees across the border into India. The crisis posed both a moral obligation and a strategic imperative. Amidst growing pressure to respond immediately, India's Army Chief General Sam Manekshaw presented a pivotal moment of leadership: he told Prime Minister Indira Gandhi bluntly in April 1971 that the Army was not ready, and asked for six to eight months to prepare properly. That act of professional honesty proved decisive — every one of those months was used to prepare the most thorough military campaign independent India had ever mounted.

When hostilities began on 3 December 1971 after Pakistan pre-emptively struck Indian airfields, India engaged simultaneously on three fronts — the Eastern front driving rapidly toward Dhaka, the Western front spanning from Kashmir to Rajasthan, and a naval campaign that included Operation Trident, India's first-ever missile-boat strike, which set Karachi harbour ablaze on the night of 4–5 December. In the east, the aircraft carrier INS Vikrant enforced a tight blockade of the Bay of Bengal, severing Pakistani maritime escape and resupply routes. Indian armoured and infantry columns advanced at a pace the Pakistani defence had no answer for, and Dhaka was encircled within days.

On 16 December 1971, Lieutenant General A.A.K. Niazi signed the Instrument of Surrender before Lieutenant General Jagjit Singh Aurora in Dhaka — 93,000 Pakistani soldiers laid down their arms, the largest military surrender since World War II. The new nation of Bangladesh was born, and India declared 16 December Vijay Diwas — Victory Day — in honour of its most complete and decisive military triumph. It was a victory won not just on the battlefield, but in the months of unglamorous, meticulous preparation that preceded it.

lessons.award: Padma Vibhushan, Padma Bhushan; Field Marshal (1973)
lessons.value: Meticulous preparation over political pressure — refused to rush to war
lessons.hook: When the Prime Minister of India ordered him to go to war, he said no — and that one act of professional honesty won India its greatest military victory.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Sam Hormusji Framji Jamshedji Manekshaw was born on 3 April 1914 in Amritsar to a Parsi family with roots in Valsad, Gujarat. His father, a doctor who had settled in Punjab, raised him with a sense of discipline and clear purpose. Commissioned from the Indian Military Academy, Dehradun, in 1934 into the 12th Frontier Force Regiment and later transferred to the 8th Gorkha Rifles, Manekshaw quickly proved himself a soldier of exceptional competence and personal courage. In the Burma campaign of World War II, he was shot five times and left for dead on the battlefield — he survived, was awarded the Military Cross, and the British general who operated on him reportedly said that only a Pathan could have survived such wounds. 'I am not a Pathan,' Manekshaw replied, 'I am a Parsi.' It was an early, characteristic act of irreverence toward authority — the quality of a man who said what was true rather than what was convenient, and it would define him for the rest of his life.

By the time of the 1971 crisis, Manekshaw was Chief of Army Staff. When the refugee exodus from East Pakistan erupted in April 1971 and Prime Minister Indira Gandhi demanded an immediate military response, Manekshaw attended a Cabinet meeting and said, bluntly, that the Army was not ready. He told the Prime Minister that if she ordered him to go to war that week, India would be defeated. He presented a precise list of what was needed — bridging equipment, artillery reserves, tank overhauling, accumulated logistics — and asked for six to eight months. Indira Gandhi, to her great credit, agreed. Manekshaw used every one of those months to prepare the most thorough military campaign India had ever mounted. He coordinated all three services, planned the simultaneous three-front strategy, and worked with the Mukti Bahini (Bangladesh freedom fighters) to pre-position intelligence and support networks inside East Pakistan.

The war began on 3 December 1971 and was over in thirteen days. The surrender of 93,000 Pakistani troops on 16 December 1971 was the most complete victory in South Asian military history. In 1973, in recognition of his outstanding service across five wars spanning four decades, Sam Manekshaw was elevated to the rank of Field Marshal — a five-star rank that had never before been awarded in independent India — the first Field Marshal the nation had ever produced. (Field Marshal K.M. Cariappa would receive the same honour in 1986, becoming the second.) He retired to Wellington in the Nilgiris and lived simply, remaining sharp and outspoken until the very end. He died on 27 June 2008, aged 94, mourned by the nation he had served so completely.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Manekshaw's insistence on proper preparation before the 1971 war is one of the most instructive episodes in modern Indian civil-military relations. By refusing to yield to political pressure and providing an honest professional assessment of the Army's readiness, he ensured that when India did fight, it fought with decisive superiority rather than improvised desperation. His approach also required moral courage of a different kind — the willingness to be seen as obstructive, cautious, even defiant, at a moment of national emergency. The result — a 13-day war, 93,000 prisoners, and the birth of a sovereign nation — validated his judgement completely. His leadership demonstrated that a military commander's highest duty is not blind compliance but honest counsel: telling the political leadership what it needs to hear, not what it wants to hear. As the first Field Marshal in independent Indian history — a rank subsequently bestowed on Field Marshal K.M. Cariappa in 1986 — he represents the pinnacle of what the Indian Army can produce: a soldier who was as effective in a Cabinet room as he was on a battlefield.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Sam Manekshaw is remembered as 'Sam Bahadur' — Brave Sam — a nickname given to him by his beloved Gorkha soldiers, and it became the title of a 2023 biographical film starring Vicky Kaushal. The Manekshaw Centre in New Delhi, the Army's premier conference facility, is named after him. He is quoted, studied, and celebrated for his wit as much as his generalship — perhaps most memorably for the line, 'If a man says he is not afraid of dying, he is either lying or he is a Gorkha.' His story is a lesson in the power of professional integrity, preparation over impulse, and the rare courage it takes to speak inconvenient truths to those in power. He participated in five wars — World War II, Kashmir in 1947, the 1962 war with China, the 1965 war with Pakistan, and the 1971 campaign that became his masterwork — and emerged from each one with his reputation and his principles intact. In a country that often celebrates those who give the right answers, Sam Manekshaw's greatest contribution was the willingness to give an honest one.

lessons.reflection: Manekshaw told the Prime Minister he wasn't ready — a rare act of professional honesty under enormous political pressure. What does it cost a leader to speak inconvenient truths to power, and what does it cost when they don't? Have you ever had to tell someone in authority something they did not want to hear? What happened, and what did it take?

What did Manekshaw tell Prime Minister Indira Gandhi when she pressed him to go to war in April 1971?
options: He said the Army was ready and could attack within a week
He said he was not ready and needed six to eight months to prepare properly
He said war was unnecessary and recommended a diplomatic solution

quiz.question: What nickname did Manekshaw's Gorkha soldiers give him, which later became the title of a biographical film?
To what rank was Sam Manekshaw promoted in 1973, making him the first person to hold that rank in independent India?
In which year was Sam Manekshaw promoted to the rank of Field Marshal?

lessons.value: Refused to bail out of a burning tank — fought until fatally hit
lessons.hook: His Centurion tank was on fire, his commanding officer was ordering him out over the radio — and he refused. 'My tank is still operational, Sir. I am not abandoning it.'

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Arun Khetarpal was born on 14 October 1950 in Pune to Brigadier M.L. Khetarpal and his wife Indira — a military family in which service was both tradition and expectation. Commissioned into the 17 Poona Horse, one of India's finest and oldest armoured regiments, on 13 June 1971, he arrived at his regiment as a fresh young officer who had barely completed his basic training. He was 21 years old.

On 16 December 1971 — the very day Pakistan was signing the Instrument of Surrender in Dhaka — ferocious tank battles were still raging on the Western front in the Shakargarh salient, Punjab. Khetarpal's troop was in the thick of the Battle of Basantar, one of the largest armoured engagements of the entire war. When his Centurion tank was struck by Pakistani anti-tank fire and caught fire, his commanding officer immediately ordered him to bail out. Khetarpal's response, transmitted over the radio, has become one of the most quoted lines in the history of the Indian Army: 'My tank is still operational, Sir. I am not abandoning it.' He drove the fight forward, destroying several more Pakistani tanks before he was hit again — this time fatally. He died inside his tank. He had been a commissioned officer for just over six months.

The Pakistani tank commander who faced him that day, who later rose to the rank of Brigadier Nisar Ahmad, visited India years afterward. He paid public tribute to Khetarpal, saying he had never in his entire military career faced a braver or more determined soldier. It was a rare and extraordinary salute across the borders of two nations that had fought each other hard.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The Battle of Basantar was critical to securing India's strategic gains on the Western front. The Shakargarh salient was the most heavily defended piece of Pakistani-held territory in the Punjab sector — its armoured defenders were tasked with preventing Indian forces from outflanking and collapsing the Pakistani line. The battle, fought at the very same moment as the surrender ceremony in Dhaka, ensured that Pakistan had no remaining military option in the west either. Khetarpal's refusal to abandon his burning tank kept the momentum of the Indian attack alive at a moment when it could have faltered. His Param Vir Chakra citation credits him with the destruction of at least four enemy tanks in his final engagement alone. He is also part of a proud regimental lineage: his regiment mentor, Lieutenant Colonel A.B. Tarapore, won the Param Vir Chakra with the same 17 Poona Horse in the 1965 war — a fact that speaks to something enduring about the spirit of that regiment.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Second Lieutenant Arun Khetarpal occupies a place of permanent honour within the 17 Poona Horse — his portrait and the full citation of his Param Vir Chakra are displayed at the regimental centre, and every 16 December the regiment marks Vijay Diwas as a dual commemoration: of India's greatest military victory, and of the youngest officer who died on that same day with his tank still fighting. His father, Brigadier M.L. Khetarpal, performed an act of extraordinary dignity — he donated his son's awarded Param Vir Chakra back to the regiment, so that it would remain permanently with the soldiers his son had served beside. The medal belongs to the Poona Horse. The story belongs to India. It appears in military training syllabi across the country as the definitive example of what it means when a soldier's sense of duty is stronger than his instinct for survival — a bond that not even a burning tank could break.

lessons.reflection: Arun Khetarpal had been commissioned for just over six months — barely enough time to learn his regiment's traditions, let alone face death. He refused to bail out of a burning tank. Was that trained courage, or something beyond training? What separates discipline from conviction? Is there a moment in your own life where duty and instinct have pulled you in exactly the same direction, whatever the cost?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Second Lieutenant Arun Khetarpal serve during the 1971 war?
What was the name of the battle in which Arun Khetarpal was killed on 16 December 1971?
What did Khetarpal say when his commanding officer ordered him to bail out of his burning tank?
options: 'I will bail out only when the crew is safe, Sir.'
'My tank is still operational, Sir. I am not abandoning it.'
'The enemy is 50 yards from us. I shall not withdraw.'
'Send reinforcements — we can still hold the line.'

quiz.question: How old was Second Lieutenant Arun Khetarpal when he was killed in action at the Battle of Basantar?
Which Pakistani officer later visited India and publicly called Khetarpal the bravest soldier he had ever faced?

lessons.hero: Flying Officer Nirmal Jit Singh Sekhon
lessons.award: Param Vir Chakra (Posthumous) — only IAF recipient
lessons.value: Took off alone into impossible odds — the only IAF officer to earn the Param Vir Chakra
lessons.hook: Six Pakistani jets were attacking Srinagar airfield. Every other aircraft in the squadron was away on operations. He was the only one on standby — and he chose to take off alone into the middle of them.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Nirmal Jit Singh Sekhon was born on 17 July 1943 in Issewal village near Ludhiana, Punjab, into a Sikh farming family. He was commissioned into the Indian Air Force in 1967 and posted to 18 Squadron, nicknamed 'Flying Bullets', which flew the Folland Gnat — a compact, agile interceptor that IAF pilots had proudly nicknamed 'Sabre Slayer' for its formidable record against Pakistan's American-supplied F-86 Sabre fighters in the 1965 war. Sekhon had trained on this aircraft and knew its capabilities intimately.

On the morning of 14 December 1971, just two days before Pakistan's final surrender, the Pakistan Air Force launched a strike against Srinagar airfield — a key installation in the Kashmir valley — using six F-86 Sabre jets operating in two waves. Most of 18 Squadron's aircraft were away on operations elsewhere in the valley. Sekhon was on readiness duty at the airfield — the standby pilot, designated to scramble at short notice if needed. When the air raid siren sounded and six enemy aircraft were confirmed inbound, Sekhon did not wait. He was told the odds were impossible. He taxied his Gnat to the runway and took off alone anyway.

What followed was one of the most courageous and unequal aerial engagements in Indian military history. Flying alone against six opponents in hostile airspace, Sekhon manoeuvred his Gnat aggressively, disrupted the formation of the attacking aircraft, shot down one Sabre confirmed, and damaged at least one more before his own aircraft was hit and he was killed. His body and his aircraft were never recovered from the mountain terrain around the airfield. He was 28 years old and, by several accounts, engaged to be married.

He was posthumously awarded the Param Vir Chakra — the only officer of the Indian Air Force ever to receive it, in any war, for any action. His name is now synonymous, within the IAF, with the understanding that courage is not the absence of fear or the calculation that victory is possible — it is the decision to act regardless.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Sekhon's single-aircraft intercept disrupted the Pakistani strike on Srinagar airfield sufficiently to degrade the attack and prevent it from achieving its objectives. In the context of the Kashmir air war in its final days, keeping Srinagar airfield operational was of real strategic value — and Sekhon's intervention contributed directly to that outcome. But the deeper impact was on morale. The story of one IAF pilot, alone against six, spread rapidly through the service and became one of the defining images of the entire 1971 air campaign — proof that the spirit of the Indian Air Force was not contingent on numbers or odds. More broadly, Sekhon's story challenges all of us to think about what readiness truly means: not just the readiness of aircraft and ordnance, but the readiness of character — the state of being prepared, in the decisive moment, to act.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Flying Officer Nirmal Jit Singh Sekhon is honoured at the IAF base in Srinagar and at Air Force Station Halwara near Ludhiana, the base closest to his home. A bust of Sekhon stands at the National War Memorial in New Delhi, among the nation's highest gallantry awardees. 18 Squadron continues to fly today, and his name is carried within the squadron as its gold standard — the bar against which all courage is measured. He is the only officer of the Indian Air Force ever to receive the Param Vir Chakra — in a service that has fought in every major Indian conflict and produced some of the most skilled fighter pilots in the world, that singular distinction speaks to the nature of what Sekhon did on the morning of 14 December 1971. He was not the most senior officer present. He was not the most experienced pilot in the squadron. He was simply the one who was there, and who chose not to look away.

lessons.reflection: Sekhon was on standby — he could have stayed grounded and no one could have blamed him. Instead he took off alone into odds no training exercise could have prepared him for. What inner voice compels a person to act when the cost is everything and the choice is entirely their own? Have you ever stepped forward when you could just as easily have stepped back?

quiz.question: In which IAF squadron did Flying Officer Nirmal Jit Singh Sekhon serve during the 1971 war?
Which aircraft did Flying Officer Sekhon fly in his lone intercept on 14 December 1971?
Which airfield did Flying Officer Sekhon defend alone against six Pakistani Sabre jets?
What is the gallantry award received posthumously by Flying Officer Sekhon, and what makes it historically unique?
options: Vir Chakra — the highest award given to an IAF pilot in the 1971 war
Maha Vir Chakra — the first ever awarded to an IAF officer
Param Vir Chakra — the only time it has ever been awarded to an IAF officer
Ashoka Chakra — the highest peacetime gallantry award

quiz.question: How old was Flying Officer Sekhon when he was killed in action on 14 December 1971?

lessons.value: Charged two bunkers alone with a bayonet — gave his life to save his company
lessons.hook: Wounded, under fire, with his company pinned down — he picked up his bayonet and charged not one enemy bunker, but two.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Albert Ekka was born on 27 December 1942 in Jari village, Gumla district, in what was then Bihar and is now Jharkhand. He was a member of the Oraon tribal community — one of India's ancient Adivasi peoples — and a Roman Catholic. He grew up in modest circumstances in the Chota Nagpur plateau, a region that has sent soldiers to the Indian Army for generations despite being one of the most economically marginalised parts of the country. Ekka enlisted in 14 Guards and was a Lance Naik — a junior non-commissioned officer — by 1971.

On 3 December 1971, the very day India entered the war, Ekka's battalion was ordered to assault the Pakistani stronghold at Gangasagar in present-day Bangladesh, on the Eastern front. The advance stalled almost immediately: a well-fortified Pakistani bunker opened heavy automatic fire, pinning down the entire company and causing casualties. With the advance stopped and men dying around him, Ekka acted without orders and without hesitation. He charged the bunker alone, silenced it with his bayonet — killing its occupants in close-quarters combat — and enabled the company to move forward.

As the company advanced, a second bunker opened fire. Already wounded from the first action, Ekka charged it too. He bayoneted the crew of the second bunker as well, but was hit mortally by fire as he did so. He died inside the second bunker, his body found alongside those of the enemy soldiers he had killed. He was 28 years old. The company he had saved went on to take its objective.

Albert Ekka was posthumously awarded the Param Vir Chakra. The Albert Ekka Chowk — a central traffic circle in Ranchi — bears his name today, ensuring that the capital of his home state remembers him.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Ekka's action at Gangasagar was decisive at the tactical level: his destruction of the first bunker broke a defensive position that had stopped an entire company's advance. The Eastern front in 1971 depended on speed — every hour that Indian columns were held up gave Pakistan time to regroup and possibly request international intervention. Acts of individual courage like Ekka's, repeated across dozens of engagements in Bangladesh, are what allowed the Eastern command to reach Dhaka in just 13 days. His Param Vir Chakra is a recognition that the decisive moments of great wars are often decided not by generals but by lance naiks who refuse to let their comrades die.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Albert Ekka's story carries a meaning beyond military valour. He came from one of the most marginalised communities in India — a tribal, rural, working-class Catholic from the Chota Nagpur plateau — and he died for a country that has not always done right by communities like his. Yet he gave everything without reservation. His story challenges every citizen to ask what their own relationship to duty and nation is, and whether it is as unconditional as his was. The Albert Ekka Chowk in Ranchi, the memorial in Gumla, and the perpetuation of his name in 14 Guards ensure that the India he defended remembers him. He is a reminder that India belongs to all its people — and that all its people have always stood for it.

lessons.reflection: Albert Ekka came from one of India's smallest, most marginalised tribal communities and died fighting for the entire nation. What does this tell us about who India belongs to? And what does it demand of those who benefit from the freedom soldiers like him secured?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Lance Naik Albert Ekka serve during the 1971 war?
From which state (as constituted today) did Lance Naik Albert Ekka hail?
To which tribal community did Albert Ekka belong?
At which location in the 1971 Eastern front did Albert Ekka perform his act of supreme valour?
What gallantry award did Lance Naik Albert Ekka receive posthumously for his actions on 3 December 1971?

lessons.rank: Major (at time of battle); later promoted to Colonel
lessons.value: Held the Basantar bridgehead through multiple armoured counter-attacks — wounded but refused evacuation till ceasefire
lessons.hook: His company held a bridgehead across the Basantar river. Pakistani tanks and infantry attacked repeatedly. He was seriously wounded. He went from trench to trench, refusing evacuation, holding his men together: not one step back.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Hoshiar Singh was born on 5 May 1936 in Sisana village of Haryana, into a family with deep roots in the farming heartland of the state — a part of India that has produced soldiers for the Indian Army across generations. He was commissioned into the 3 Grenadiers Regiment, one of the Indian Army's oldest and most battle-tested infantry regiments, and in 1963 was first posted to the North East Frontier Agency (NEFA), gaining early experience on India's most demanding frontiers. He served as a young officer in the 1965 war, accumulating the tactical instincts and professional depth that would prove decisive six years later.

On 15 December 1971, in the climactic phase of the 1971 war, 3 Grenadiers were assigned a task of the highest strategic importance: to establish a bridgehead across the Basantar river in the Shakargarh Sector of Punjab — a crossing that would allow Indian forces to drive deep into Pakistan's defensive heartland. Major Hoshiar Singh was ordered to capture the enemy locality of Jarpal, a heavily fortified position held by Pakistani infantry and armour. Under withering artillery and tank fire, Major Singh's company fought its way forward, seized the objective, and held the bridgehead despite devastating, sustained shelling.

What followed over the next two days was even more gruelling than the initial assault. Recognising the strategic danger, the Pakistani Army launched a series of coordinated counter-attacks — armour and infantry together, wave after wave — designed to push the Indians back across the Basantar and restore their defensive line. Major Hoshiar Singh was seriously wounded in the fighting. He refused evacuation. Bleeding and in pain, he went from trench to trench — steadying his men, repositioning defenders, and personally manning a machine gun when the line demanded it. He remained in command until the ceasefire was declared.

Hoshiar Singh was awarded the Param Vir Chakra — the nation's highest gallantry award — for sustained bravery, exceptional tactical judgement, and the kind of devotion to duty that cannot be ordered. He eventually retired from the army as Colonel.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The Shakargarh Sector was one of the most strategically contested axes on the Western front in the 1971 war. Pakistan's defence of the Basantar river line was built to prevent Indian forces from breaching the Shakargarh bulge — a Pakistani salient whose collapse would have allowed India to threaten Sialkot and unravel Pakistan's entire defensive posture in Punjab. By establishing and then holding the Jarpal bridgehead against repeated, determined armoured counter-attacks, Major Hoshiar Singh and the men of 3 Grenadiers denied Pakistan any hope of restoring its defensive line, directly accelerating the collapse of Pakistani resistance in the sector. His refusal to accept evacuation despite serious wounds — staying to command through two continuous days of sustained assault — is cited in the Indian Army as a defining example of what officer leadership looks like under the hardest conditions.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Colonel Hoshiar Singh is honoured at the Grenadiers Regimental Centre and remembered across Haryana, a state that has produced a remarkable proportion of India's finest soldiers across every conflict. His story stands out for the combination of qualities it illustrates: the tactical sharpness required to seize a fortified objective under fire, the steadiness of character needed to hold it against sustained armoured counter-attack, and the personal courage to remain wounded at one's post when evacuation would have been completely justified. He was also, in his peacetime life, an accomplished volleyball player who represented Punjab at the national level — a detail worth holding onto, because it is a reminder that the men who achieve the highest honours in war are, in ordinary life, whole and varied human beings. Hoshiar Singh was a farmer's son from Haryana, a Grenadiers officer, a volleyball player, and a Param Vir Chakra — all at once.

lessons.reflection: Hoshiar Singh was seriously wounded but refused evacuation — he stayed to command his men through two days of repeated armoured counter-attacks. Is that self-sacrifice, or stubbornness? How does a leader know, in the thick of it, that staying is the right decision and not just pride refusing to yield? And does the distinction even matter, in the end, if the men around you need you there?

To lead a flanking armoured column toward the Shakargarh town

He refused evacuation and continued commanding his company until the ceasefire

modules.title: Heights of Courage — The Kargil War
modules.focus: In the summer of 1999, Pakistani forces secretly seized Indian peaks above 15,000 feet — Indian soldiers had to claw them back by climbing near-vertical cliff faces under fire, in the highest-altitude battlefield in modern military history.
modules.activity: Look at photographs of Tiger Hill, Tololing, or Point 5140 and note the sheer gradient and exposure of the terrain. Write one paragraph describing what you think it would feel like — physically and mentally — to climb that face in darkness while under fire from above.
modules.challenge: Choose one of the four Param Vir Chakra recipients from this module. Write a letter to them as if you were a fellow soldier who watched what they did. Do not describe their action — tell them, honestly, what their choice means to you personally.
modules.summary:
In the winter of 1998–99, Pakistani soldiers and militants secretly occupied a string of commanding heights in the Kargil-Drass sector of Jammu & Kashmir — peaks ranging from 15,000 to nearly 18,000 feet — in an operation known as Operation Badr, designed to sever the Srinagar-Leh highway, isolate the Siachen Glacier, and force India into negotiations over Kashmir from a position of strategic disadvantage. The infiltration was discovered by Indian Army patrols in early May 1999, and the government launched Operation Vijay — a determination to recapture every occupied peak, however steep, however fortified.

The terrain that Indian soldiers faced was unlike any battlefield in modern military history: near-vertical cliffs, boulders the size of buildings, zero natural cover, and defenders who could observe every approaching soldier from the moment they left their base camps. Attacks had to be launched uphill, often at night, carrying weapons and ammunition at altitudes where each breath required twice the effort. Major engagements unfolded across the Drass, Mushkoh, Kaksar, and Batalik sub-sectors — Tololing, Tiger Hill, Point 5140, Point 4875, and the Khalubar-Jubar complex — each requiring individual acts of extraordinary personal courage to break through Pakistani positions that held every tactical advantage. The Indian Air Force launched Operation Safed Sagar — the first significant use of fixed-wing air power in the high Himalayas — deploying MiG-21, MiG-27, and Mirage 2000 aircraft in strike and close air support roles, suffering early losses before adapting tactics to the extreme altitude, thin air, and unforgiving terrain.

Sustained international pressure — particularly from the United States — combined with India's relentless military advances, forced Pakistan to order a withdrawal. Pakistani forces began pulling back from 11 July, and India declared complete victory on 26 July 1999, a date celebrated annually as Kargil Vijay Diwas. Four soldiers were awarded the Param Vir Chakra for the Kargil War — two posthumously and two to living heroes — the highest number of PVCs in any single conflict since 1971, a measure of the extraordinary courage the operation demanded of those who fought it.

moduleQuiz.question: What is the Indian Army's operational name for the campaign to recapture Kargil heights in 1999?
On what date is Kargil Vijay Diwas celebrated each year, marking India's declaration of complete victory?
How many Param Vir Chakras were awarded for gallantry during the Kargil War of 1999?
What famous phrase did Captain Vikram Batra radio back to headquarters immediately after capturing Point 5140?
Which peak is most directly associated with Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey's final and fatal battle in the Batalik sector?

lessons.value: Captured Point 5140, led assault on Point 4875, died pulling a wounded colleague to safety
lessons.hook: After capturing a near-impregnable peak at nearly 17,000 feet in darkness, he radioed back to headquarters and declared 'Yeh Dil Maange More' — and then turned around and went back up for another peak entirely.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Vikram Batra was born on 9 September 1974 in Palampur, a small hill town in Himachal Pradesh, to Girdhari Lal Batra, a government school principal, and Kamal Kanta Batra. He was the second of twin brothers — his identical twin, Vishal. Vikram was by all accounts an energetic, warm, and fearless young man: a champion table-tennis player, a college student who charmed everyone around him, and someone whose personality filled rooms. He had been accepted into a prestigious merchant navy training programme — a lucrative career path — but declined it when he caught sight of an Indian Army officer in uniform at a selection centre and felt something shift inside him. He said later that he could not imagine doing anything else.

Commissioned into the 13 JAK Rifles in 1997 after training at the Indian Military Academy, Batra was a young lieutenant when the Kargil conflict began. His code name for the operations was 'Sher Shah'. By the time he was assigned to recapture Point 5140 — a peak in the Drass sub-sector standing at approximately 16,962 feet, held by well-entrenched Pakistani soldiers — he was a captain, and his men already knew him as a leader who led from the front. The assault on Point 5140 on the night of 19–20 June 1999 was conducted in darkness, up near-vertical rock faces swept by enemy fire. Batra directed his platoon's climb, engaged enemy positions at close quarters, and cleared the peak in a fierce firefight. When he radioed back to headquarters, he used the phrase 'Yeh Dil Maange More' — a Pepsi advertising slogan he had adapted with characteristic irreverence — and the words, broadcast on television, became the slogan of the entire war overnight, repeated by soldiers, journalists, and the public alike.

Assigned next to the assault on Point 4875 — an even more difficult objective — Batra led his company up another sheer face on 7 July 1999. The fighting was brutal and at close range. During the battle, a fellow officer, Lieutenant Naveen, was badly wounded in the open. Batra moved forward under direct fire to pull him back to cover. In that final act of going forward to retrieve his brother officer, he was hit by a burst of automatic fire and killed. He was 24 years old. His last words, according to those present, were 'Jai Mata Di.'

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The fall of Point 5140 — and Batra's exuberant radio message — was a turning point in the public experience of the Kargil War. India was watching the conflict unfold on television in real time, often with anxiety and grief; Batra's voice, his youth, and his bravado gave the nation something it badly needed: a face and a voice for its soldiers' courage. His death, coming so soon after while saving a wounded fellow officer, made that image permanent and devastating in equal measure. He was awarded the Param Vir Chakra posthumously — India's highest gallantry decoration. The government of Himachal Pradesh named a stretch of the Manali-Leh highway in his honour, and numerous schools, roads, and institutions across India carry his name. His parents — his father Girdhari Lal Batra and mother Kamal Kanta Batra — with extraordinary grace, accepted his death as the price their son had chosen to pay and have spent decades keeping his story alive for the nation.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Vikram Batra's twin brother Vishal was deeply affected by his brother's death — a loss that shaped the rest of his life, even as he pursued his own path outside the military. The 2021 Bollywood film Shershaah — Vikram's code name during the Kargil operations — brought his story to an entirely new generation of Indians and became one of the most-watched films of the year. But beyond the films and the memorials, his legacy lives in the simplest terms: he was a young man who had options, chose service, and when the final moment came, chose someone else's life over his own. 'Yeh Dil Maange More' is no longer an advertisement. It is a statement about what it means to give everything and still want to give more.

lessons.reflection: Vikram Batra was pulling a wounded fellow officer to safety when he was killed — the last act of his life was going forward for someone else. How do we carry a person's story forward after they're gone, and does the telling ever do justice to what they did? What does it mean to choose another person's life over your own in the final moment?

What did Captain Batra say over the radio immediately after his company captured Point 5140?
options: Jai Hind — the peak is ours
Na Chhodnu — don't let them escape

quiz.question: What was Captain Vikram Batra doing when he was fatally shot on Point 4875 on 7 July 1999?
options: Leading the final assault on an enemy bunker
Radioing a situation report to headquarters

Planting the Indian flag on the peak

quiz.question: What gallantry award was Captain Vikram Batra given for his actions in the Kargil War?

lessons.value: Cleared four fortified enemy bunkers personally in the Batalik sector before being killed at the last
lessons.hook: He wrote in his diary that some goals are so worthy it is glorious even to fail — then he went to Khalubar, was wounded twice, cleared four bunkers alone, and proved he had meant every word.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Manoj Kumar Pandey was born on 25 June 1975 in Sitapur, a district in Uttar Pradesh, into a middle-class family. From childhood, he was fixed on a single ambition: to become an Indian Army officer. He was disciplined, studious, and deeply motivated, and those who knew him remembered that his commitment to the army was not a young man's passing fantasy but a considered, passionate vocation. He kept a diary, and in it he wrote a sentence that would become his most quoted words after his death: 'Some goals are so worthy, it is glorious even to fail.' He was commissioned in June 1997 into the 1/11 Gorkha Rifles — one of the most decorated Gorkha battalions in the Indian Army — and within two years, he was being tested in conditions none of his training could fully have prepared him for.

The Batalik sector of Kargil was the most remote and perhaps the most heavily fortified of the Pakistani-held positions. The Khalubar and Jubar hills — collectively known as Khalubar Top — were defended by dug-in machine-gun posts, rocket launchers, and well-supplied troops who held every advantage of elevation and observation. On 3 July 1999, Lieutenant Pandey was ordered to lead his Gorkha platoon in an assault on these heights. The approach required climbing near-vertical rock faces under sustained machine-gun and rocket fire. Pandey led from the front throughout. He personally assaulted and cleared the first enemy bunker, sustaining a wound from grenade fragments. He continued, cleared the second bunker, was wounded a second time, and continued still. At the third bunker, his men were pinned — Pandey moved alone to flank the position and destroyed it. Approaching the fourth and final bunker, he turned to his men and shouted 'Na Chhodnu' — in Nepali, 'don't leave them' or 'don't let them escape.' Those were the last words his soldiers heard from him. He was killed clearing that final position. He was 24 years old.

Pandey was posthumously promoted to the rank of Captain and awarded the Param Vir Chakra. His actions on Khalubar that day opened the route for Indian forces to secure the entire sub-sector.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The Batalik sub-sector was among the last major areas of Pakistani occupation to be cleared, and Khalubar was one of its most formidable positions. Pandey's willingness to press forward after two separate wounds — alone, against fortified bunkers at altitude — exemplified the spirit that made Operation Vijay a complete Indian victory across every sub-sector. His Param Vir Chakra citation noted that his actions inspired his men to clear the position and secure the Batalik sector. For the families of soldiers in Uttar Pradesh — a state that contributes enormously to the Indian Army — he became a symbol that sacrifice and duty were not the preserve of famous names or privileged regiments: a young man from Sitapur could stand with the greatest heroes the republic has ever produced.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey is honoured at the National War Memorial in New Delhi, his name inscribed alongside every Indian soldier who has fallen in the service of the nation since 1947. A statue stands in Sitapur; roads and schools across Uttar Pradesh carry his name. His regiment — 1/11 Gorkha Rifles — honours him at every regimental gathering. His diary entry — 'some goals are so worthy, it is glorious even to fail' — is quoted in military academies, schools, and institutions as a statement about why we pursue difficult things at all. He did not fail: he was killed clearing the last bunker, having done everything the mission asked of him and more. But what his diary entry really teaches is that the willingness to risk failure for something truly worthy is itself a form of greatness — whether or not victory arrives.

lessons.reflection: Pandey wrote 'some goals are so worthy it is glorious even to fail.' He was killed pursuing exactly that kind of goal. What does this tell us about the relationship between purpose and outcome? Is there a goal in your own life worthy enough to pursue even knowing you might not succeed — and what does that say about who you want to be?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey serve during the Kargil War?
In which sub-sector of Kargil did Captain Pandey lead his final assault on 3 July 1999?
What did Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey write in his personal diary that became his most famous words?
options: Death is not the end — service is eternal

quiz.question: What were the last words Captain Pandey's soldiers heard from him as he approached the final enemy bunker at Khalubar?
