lessons.rank: Naib Subedar (Rifleman at time of action)
lessons.value: Shot twice, fought on — cleared enemy positions single-handed at Flat Top (Point 4875)
lessons.hook: Shot in the forearm. Kept running. Shot in the chest. Kept fighting. He is alive today, and the memory of what he did lives in his body.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Sanjay Kumar was born on 3 March 1976 in Bakain village, Bilaspur district, Himachal Pradesh — a small mountain community not far from Palampur, the birthplace of his regiment-mate Vikram Batra. He enlisted as a Rifleman in the 13 JAK Rifles, the same battalion in which Captain Batra served, and by July 1999 was part of the Indian Army's push to recapture the heights of the Mushkoh Valley.

On 4 July 1999, during the assault on the feature known as Area Flat Top — a component of the Point 4875 complex — Sanjay Kumar's company came under withering automatic fire from enemy positions that had pinned down the entire advance. Unable to move, the company was in danger of taking catastrophic casualties. Sanjay Kumar made a decision that no order required of him: he rose from cover and charged the enemy position alone. He was shot through the forearm almost immediately. He did not stop. Continuing his charge, he reached the first enemy bunker, killed three Pakistani soldiers in close hand-to-hand combat, and destroyed the position. He then moved to assault a second bunker. He was shot again — this time in the chest. He cleared the second bunker despite this wound, enabling his company to resume its advance. His actions broke the deadlock and were directly instrumental in India's recapture of Flat Top.

Sanjay Kumar survived both wounds and was evacuated, treated, and recovered. He was promoted over the years to the rank of Naib Subedar. He is one of only four living Param Vir Chakra recipients in India today — a distinction that makes him a living embodiment of the standard the award represents.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The PVC is most often discussed as a posthumous award — the majority of its recipients died in the act of earning it. Sanjay Kumar's survival makes his story distinctive: he carries in his body the permanent record of what he did. His existence as a living recipient means that the award's meaning can be communicated in person, in schools and barracks and ceremonies, by the man himself. His continued service after Kargil — rising through the ranks — demonstrated that the act of extraordinary courage was not a single isolated moment but the expression of a character that continued to serve. The army has used his testimony and presence in recruitment and training contexts to illustrate what the values of service and courage look like in practice.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Naib Subedar Sanjay Kumar is one of the Indian Army's most tangible connections to the Kargil War — not a name on a memorial, but a man who appears in uniform at functions, speaks to young soldiers, and represents the living continuity of the PVC tradition. His home district of Bilaspur has honoured him, and his regiment maintains his citation prominently. For students and young people, he is perhaps the most direct reminder that extraordinary courage is not a property of the past — it is something that a living person, still walking among us, chose to exercise on a July morning on a Himalayan peak.

lessons.reflection: Sanjay Kumar was shot twice and kept fighting. He is alive today and carries those memories — in his body and his mind. What is the difference between reading about courage and having lived it? What does it mean to be a living witness to your own most extreme moment?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Sanjay Kumar serve when he performed his act of gallantry in the Kargil War?
At which location did Sanjay Kumar perform his gallant action on 4 July 1999?
How many times was Sanjay Kumar shot during his assault on the enemy positions at Flat Top?
options: Twice — in the forearm and in the chest
Three times — in both legs and the arm
He was not shot but was wounded by grenade fragments

quiz.question: What gallantry award did Sanjay Kumar receive for his actions during the Kargil War?
What makes Naib Subedar Sanjay Kumar's Param Vir Chakra especially notable compared to most other PVC recipients?
options: He was the youngest person ever to receive it
He received it for a peacetime act of bravery
He survived and is a living recipient of India's highest gallantry award
He was the first non-commissioned officer to receive the award

lessons.award: Param Vir Chakra (survived — youngest-ever PVC recipient at age 19)
lessons.value: Shot three times, climbed Tiger Hill alone, destroyed three bunkers — the youngest person ever to receive the Param Vir Chakra
lessons.hook: The rope-climbing assault party was nearly wiped out before it reached the top. He was 19 years old, had been shot three times, and climbed the rest of Tiger Hill himself.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Yogendra Singh Yadav was born on 10 May 1980 in Ahir village, Bulandshahr district, Uttar Pradesh. He came from a family with a strong soldiering tradition — his grandfather had served in World War II, and the army was a natural path for a young man from a community that had always given soldiers to India. He enlisted in the 18 Grenadiers and by 1999, at the age of 19, was a Grenadier — the rank equivalent of a private — with barely a year of service behind him. He was, in every sense, a new soldier.

On the night of 3–4 July 1999, the 18 Grenadiers were tasked with assaulting Tiger Hill — the most strategically important and most heavily fortified peak of the entire Kargil War. Tiger Hill dominated the Srinagar-Leh highway and had been the centrepiece of Pakistan's defensive plan in the Drass sector. The assault required climbing a near-vertical cliff face in darkness while Pakistani troops above hurled grenades and poured automatic-weapon fire down the slope. Yogendra Singh Yadav was part of the Ghatak platoon — an elite commando unit within the battalion — tasked with leading the climb. As his rope-climbing party neared the top, Pakistani soldiers detected them and opened fire with grenades and machine guns. The leading team was almost entirely wiped out in moments — most men were hit, some fell, the assault appeared finished before it had properly begun.

Yogendra Singh Yadav volunteered to continue the climb alone. He moved up the rope. He was hit by grenade fragments and three separate bullet wounds — through both legs and one arm — and he climbed on. Reaching the top, he engaged three enemy bunkers with grenades and close-quarters combat, destroying each in turn, and cleared the path for the rest of his platoon to gain the summit. His actions were the turning point that allowed Tiger Hill to fall to Indian forces. He was taken for dead by his own unit — his name was placed on the casualty list — and then he walked into the aid post alive, to the astonishment of those who had already mourned him.

Yogendra Singh Yadav was 19 years, 1 month, and 24 days old at the time — the youngest person in the history of India to be awarded the Param Vir Chakra.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The capture of Tiger Hill on 4 July 1999 was the single most strategically significant military achievement of the Kargil War, and Yogendra Singh Yadav's solo climb — wounded three times, alone after his party was cut down — was the act that made it possible. The image of a 19-year-old from Bulandshahr, barely out of basic training, climbing through his own wounds to clear three bunkers alone, represented in the most visceral possible terms the spirit the entire nation had been watching from afar. His survival — walking into the aid post after being listed among the dead — carried a power beyond any citation. He was not a famous officer. He was a Grenadier, a private. And he had done something that would have been extraordinary even for the most seasoned soldier in the army.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Yogendra Singh Yadav remained in the Indian Army after Kargil, rising through the non-commissioned officer ranks and eventually retiring as a Subedar Major. He is a living recipient of the Param Vir Chakra — the youngest in the award's entire history — and one of the most recognisable faces of the Kargil War for a generation of Indians who were too young to remember it themselves. He has spoken at schools, military institutions, and public events across India, bringing the reality of the Kargil heights to audiences who were not alive in 1999. His grandfather's service in World War II flows through him — the unbroken thread of ordinary men from ordinary villages who have always stood between India and its adversaries, not because they were born different, but because they chose to be.

lessons.reflection: Yogendra was 19 years old — the same age many people start college. He had been shot three times, the assault party around him was gone, and he had every reason to stop. He climbed the rest of the way alone. What does a story like this tell us about the outer limits of what the human body and will can endure? And after hearing it, what does the word 'ordinary' really mean?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Grenadier Yogendra Singh Yadav serve during the assault on Tiger Hill?
How old was Yogendra Singh Yadav when he performed his gallant action at Tiger Hill in July 1999?
What happened to the leading rope-climbing assault party as it neared the top of Tiger Hill?
options: It was turned back by extreme weather conditions
It ran out of ammunition before reaching the summit
It was nearly wiped out by Pakistani grenades and machine-gun fire
It was ordered to withdraw and regroup for a second attempt

quiz.question: What gallantry award did Grenadier Yogendra Singh Yadav receive for his actions at Tiger Hill?
What unique record does Yogendra Singh Yadav hold in relation to the Param Vir Chakra?
options: He received it for a peacetime act of bravery
He is the only person to receive two Param Vir Chakras
He is the youngest person ever to receive the Param Vir Chakra
He was the first soldier from a Grenadier regiment to receive it

lessons.value: Shot down over Pakistan, held as POW for 8 days — maintained composure and gave only name, rank, and number in captivity
lessons.hook: His plane went down over enemy territory. He was alone, captured, and interrogated for 8 days. He came home with his dignity completely intact.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
K.K. Nachiketa was born in 1969 and joined the Indian Air Force as a fighter pilot, eventually flying the MiG-27 ground-attack aircraft. The MiG-27, known in IAF service as the Bahadur (Brave), was one of the primary platforms used in Operation Safed Sagar — the IAF's contribution to Operation Vijay — conducting close air support and strike missions against Pakistani positions on the mountain peaks. Operating at extreme altitudes with limited engine performance margins, the aircraft were vulnerable to both terrain and technical failures in ways that low-altitude missions over plains would not present.

On 27 May 1999 — the earliest days of the IAF's involvement in the Kargil campaign — Nachiketa was flying a MiG-27 strike sortie over Pakistani-occupied territory when the aircraft suffered a technical malfunction. The engine flamed out, and the aircraft became uncontrollable. He ejected successfully, but his ejection seat carried him down into Pakistani-controlled territory, where he was captured by Pakistani forces within hours of landing. He was taken into Pakistani military custody and became India's first prisoner of war since the 1971 conflict.

Over the next 8 days, Nachiketa was held in Pakistani custody, subjected to interrogation, and made available to Pakistani media — his appearance on television became a significant episode in the information war that accompanied the military conflict. In all interactions with his captors and before the cameras, he maintained strict adherence to the Geneva Convention code of conduct: he gave his name, rank, and number, and nothing more. His composure, his refusal to be used as a propaganda instrument, and his quiet dignity throughout captivity were widely noted. He was repatriated to India on 3 June 1999 under diplomatic pressure, particularly from the United States. He returned to flying duties and continued his career in the IAF.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Nachiketa's capture and repatriation played out on Indian and international television at a moment when the Kargil conflict was being watched by the entire world. His demeanour in captivity — calm, composed, giving nothing away — stood in sharp contrast to what his captors had hoped to extract from him. The episode demonstrated that India's armed forces maintained their discipline even in the most extreme circumstances of capture and isolation. His release also marked the first significant diplomatic intervention by the United States in the conflict, signalling to the world that Pakistan's military adventure had failed on every level — military, diplomatic, and informational.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Squadron Leader Nachiketa is awarded the Vir Chakra — India's third-highest wartime gallantry award — for his conduct during the mission and in captivity. He is a reminder that the Kargil War involved every branch of the Indian armed forces, not only the army soldiers who climbed the peaks. The IAF lost aircraft and aircrew in Operation Safed Sagar, and Nachiketa's story represents those losses and that service. He also represents a dimension of military courage that is less often discussed: not the charge up a hill, but the quiet maintenance of dignity and identity when you are alone, surrounded, and have nothing to rely on but your own character.

lessons.reflection: Nachiketa was a prisoner in an enemy country for 8 days — surrounded, alone, interrogated. He could not fight his way out or call for help. All he had was his own character. What does it mean to maintain your dignity and composure when you have nothing left to rely on but yourself? Is that a different kind of courage from what we saw in the other lessons?

quiz.question: In which branch of the Indian armed forces did K.K. Nachiketa serve during the Kargil War?
Which aircraft was Squadron Leader Nachiketa flying when he was shot down on 27 May 1999?
What happened to Squadron Leader Nachiketa on 27 May 1999 during his strike mission?
options: He was shot down by a Pakistani surface-to-air missile and killed
His aircraft developed a technical fault; he ejected and was captured by Pakistani forces
He made an emergency landing at a forward airstrip in Pakistan-held territory
He was forced to abort his mission due to extreme weather over the peaks

quiz.question: How long was Squadron Leader Nachiketa held as a prisoner of war in Pakistani custody?
What gallantry award did Squadron Leader K.K. Nachiketa receive for his conduct during the Kargil War?

modules.title: Beyond Borders — Special Ops, Peacekeeping & Earlier Wars
modules.war: Multiple — WWII, Congo 1961, Siachen 1987, Sri Lanka 1987, Mumbai 2008
modules.focus: Indian soldiers have served with distinction beyond conventional wars—from the jungles of Burma and UN peacekeeping missions to the heights of Siachen, streets of Colombo, and the corridors of the Taj Hotel — each time with exceptional courage.
modules.activity: India is one of the world's largest contributors to United Nations peacekeeping missions. Research one UN mission involving Indian soldiers and write a short summary explaining its purpose, India's contribution, and the challenges faced by the peacekeepers.
modules.challenge: The heroes in this module served in very different environments—from jungles and glaciers to foreign countries and city streets. Choose the hero whose story inspired you the most and write a 100-word reflection explaining why their courage or leadership remains relevant today.
modules.summary:
India's military story extends far beyond its own borders and conventional wars. During the Second World War, over 2.5 million Indian volunteers served in the British Indian Army, creating the largest all-volunteer force in history. In the demanding Burma Campaign, Indian soldiers fought through dense jungles, rugged mountains, disease, and relentless combat against the Imperial Japanese Army. Among the distinguished commanders was Kodendera Subayya Thimayya, whose outstanding leadership earned him the Distinguished Service Order before he later became Chief of Army Staff of independent India.

After Independence, India emerged as one of the world's leading contributors to United Nations peacekeeping operations. This commitment produced one of the most remarkable acts of gallantry in UN history when Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria of 3/1 Gorkha Rifles led a small platoon in a daring assault against a heavily armed Katangese force in the Congo in December 1961. Despite overwhelming odds, he destroyed two armoured vehicles, killed approximately forty enemy soldiers in close combat, and made the supreme sacrifice, becoming the only Indian to receive the Param Vir Chakra while serving in a United Nations operation.

India's tradition of courage continued on the world's highest battlefield. In June 1987, during Operation Rajiv on the Siachen Glacier, Naib Subedar Bana Singh led a small assault team up a near-vertical ice wall at over 21,000 feet to capture the seemingly impregnable Quaid Post, which was later renamed Bana Post in his honour. Later that year, during Operation Pawan in Sri Lanka, Major Ramaswamy Parameswaran turned a deadly LTTE ambush into a decisive counterattack, continuing to lead his men even after being mortally wounded and earning a posthumous Param Vir Chakra.

The same spirit of selfless service was displayed during the 26 November 2008 Mumbai terrorist attacks. Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan of the National Security Guard led his assault team into the burning Taj Mahal Palace Hotel, rescued hostages, ensured the evacuation of his wounded colleague, and laid down his life while pursuing the terrorists. His Ashok Chakra, India's highest peacetime gallantry award, recognises a form of courage as inspiring as any displayed on the battlefield.

From the jungles of Burma to the mountains of Siachen, from UN peacekeeping operations in Africa to counter-insurgency in Sri Lanka and counter-terrorism in Mumbai, these heroes remind us that the values of courage, leadership, sacrifice, and service remain constant, no matter where duty calls.

moduleQuiz.question: Who is the only Indian soldier to receive the Param Vir Chakra while serving in a United Nations operation?
What was the original name of the Pakistani post captured by Naib Subedar Bana Singh during Operation Rajiv in 1987?
In which country did India conduct Operation Pawan, where Major Ramaswamy Parameswaran earned the Param Vir Chakra?
During the Second World War, in which campaign did General K.S. Thimayya command an infantry brigade?
Which gallantry award was Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan posthumously awarded for his actions during the 2008 Mumbai terrorist attacks?

lessons.war: World War II (Burma Campaign), 1947–48 Indo-Pak War, Korean War (UN), Cyprus (UN)
lessons.value: A distinguished battlefield commander, Chief of Army Staff, and internationally respected United Nations leader.
lessons.hook: He fought through the jungles of Burma, led Indian troops in Kashmir, became Chief of Army Staff, and died while serving the United Nations in Cyprus—a soldier whose commitment to duty never ended.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Kodendera Subayya Thimayya was born on 30 March 1906 in Madikeri (Mercara), Kodagu (Coorg), Karnataka, into a respected Kodava family with a long tradition of public service. Educated at the Prince of Wales Royal Indian Military College in Dehradun and later at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, he was commissioned into the British Indian Army in 1926 and joined the 8th Gorkha Rifles. His professionalism, courage, and concern for his soldiers soon earned him the respect of both his superiors and the Gorkha troops he commanded.

During the Second World War, Thimayya commanded the 268th Indian Infantry Brigade in the Burma Campaign, one of the longest and most demanding campaigns fought against the Imperial Japanese Army. Operating in dense jungles, mountainous terrain, monsoon conditions, and severe disease, he displayed outstanding leadership that earned him the Distinguished Service Order (DSO), one of the highest British awards for distinguished leadership in combat.

After India became independent, Thimayya played a key role during the 1947–48 Indo-Pakistani War. He commanded forces in Jammu & Kashmir and helped lead the successful operations to recapture strategic areas, including the famous Zoji La offensive, where Indian forces employed tanks at high altitude for the first time in military history. His operational skill contributed significantly to securing Ladakh and protecting India's northern frontiers.

In 1953, following the Korean War, Thimayya was appointed Chairman of the Neutral Nations Repatriation Commission, overseeing the sensitive repatriation of thousands of prisoners of war under United Nations supervision. His fairness, professionalism, and diplomatic skill earned him international respect. He became Chief of Army Staff in 1957 and served until 1961, guiding the Indian Army through a period of expansion and modernisation. During his tenure, differences emerged with Defence Minister V.K. Krishna Menon regarding military preparedness and administration. Although he briefly submitted his resignation in 1959, he withdrew it at the request of Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru, placing institutional stability above personal disagreement.

Following his retirement, Thimayya accepted another call to service as Commander of the United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus (UNFICYP). While serving in Cyprus, he suffered a heart attack and passed away on 18 December 1965. He remained on active international duty until the very end of his life.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body:
General Thimayya's career bridged the transition from the British Indian Army to the modern Indian Army. His battlefield leadership during the Burma Campaign, his command during the Kashmir operations of 1947–48, and his successful international assignments demonstrated that Indian military leadership could excel both in war and diplomacy.

As Chief of Army Staff, he strengthened professionalism, leadership development, and operational readiness during the formative years of independent India. His service with the United Nations in Korea and Cyprus further established India's reputation as a responsible contributor to international peacekeeping. Today, he is remembered not only as an outstanding battlefield commander but also as one of the architects of the professional ethos of the post-Independence Indian Army.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body:
General K.S. Thimayya is widely regarded as one of India's finest military leaders. His career demonstrated excellence in battlefield command, strategic planning, international diplomacy, and institutional leadership. Soldiers continue to study his campaigns as examples of courage, sound judgement, and calm decision-making under pressure.

His life is commemorated through military institutions, memorials, and biographies, while the Gorkha Regiment proudly preserves his legacy. In Kodagu, he remains one of the region's most celebrated sons. His service in Burma, Kashmir, Korea, and Cyprus reminds us that military leadership extends beyond winning battles—it also involves protecting peace, strengthening institutions, and serving humanity with integrity.

General Thimayya's life stands as an enduring example of professionalism, humility, and unwavering devotion to duty. Whether commanding soldiers in combat or representing India on the world stage, he consistently placed service above personal recognition.

lessons.reflection: General Thimayya served in war, peacekeeping, military leadership, and international diplomacy. Throughout his career, he placed duty above personal ambition and continued serving even after retirement. What qualities allow a leader to earn trust in both military and civilian settings? How can professionalism, integrity, and humility help us become better leaders in our own lives?

quiz.question: Which regiment did General K.S. Thimayya serve with throughout most of his military career?
During the Second World War, in which campaign did General Thimayya command an infantry brigade and earn the Distinguished Service Order (DSO)?
What important United Nations responsibility did General Thimayya undertake after the Korean War?
options: Led the Neutral Nations Repatriation Commission overseeing prisoner repatriation

quiz.question: What was the highest military appointment held by General K.S. Thimayya in independent India?
Where was General Thimayya serving when he passed away in 1965?
options: Cyprus, while commanding the United Nations Peacekeeping Force

lessons.war: United Nations Operation in the Congo (ONUC), December 1961
lessons.value: The only Indian to receive the Param Vir Chakra while serving in a United Nations peacekeeping operation.
lessons.hook: At just 26 years old, Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria led a small platoon of Gorkhas against a much larger force in the Congo. Fighting under the United Nations flag, he made the supreme sacrifice while helping restore peace in a distant land.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria was born on 29 November 1935 in Jangal village, Shakargarh Tehsil (now in Pakistan). Following the Partition of India in 1947, his family moved to Jandiala Guru in Amritsar district, Punjab. Inspired by a deep sense of patriotism, he joined the National Defence Academy and was commissioned into the 3rd Battalion, 1st Gorkha Rifles in 1957. His courage, enthusiasm, and commitment to duty quickly earned the respect of both his seniors and the Gorkha soldiers he led.

In 1960, the newly independent Republic of the Congo descended into civil conflict after the mineral-rich Katanga Province attempted to secede. The United Nations launched the United Nations Operation in the Congo (ONUC) to restore peace and maintain the country's unity. India contributed one of the largest military contingents to the mission, and Captain Salaria volunteered to serve as part of the Indian peacekeeping force.

On 5 December 1961, near Elizabethville (now Lubumbashi), Captain Salaria and his platoon were tasked with clearing a roadblock established by the Katangese Gendarmerie. The enemy force, numbering around 150–200 soldiers and supported by armoured vehicles, heavily outnumbered the Indian troops. Recognising that delay would allow the enemy to regroup, Captain Salaria boldly ordered an immediate assault. He personally destroyed two armoured vehicles using a rocket launcher before leading his Gorkhas into fierce close-quarters combat with their traditional khukris and rifles. Despite being mortally wounded by enemy fire, he continued leading the attack until the roadblock was broken and the enemy dispersed. His extraordinary courage and leadership helped secure the mission, though he made the supreme sacrifice at just 26 years of age.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body:
Captain Salaria's sacrifice demonstrated that Indian soldiers serving under the United Nations flag displayed the same courage and professionalism as they did while defending India's own borders. His actions strengthened India's reputation as one of the world's most dependable contributors to international peacekeeping and highlighted the willingness of Indian soldiers to risk their lives in the cause of global peace.

For his conspicuous bravery, inspiring leadership, and supreme sacrifice, Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria was posthumously awarded the Param Vir Chakra. He remains the only Indian to receive the nation's highest gallantry award while serving in a United Nations peacekeeping mission.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body:
Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria occupies a unique place in Indian military history. Memorials, schools, roads, and institutions have been named in his honour, while his name is engraved at the National War Memorial in New Delhi. Within the Gorkha Rifles, his story continues to inspire generations of officers and soldiers.

More importantly, his life symbolises India's long-standing commitment to international peacekeeping. He fought not to capture territory, but to protect innocent lives and uphold peace in a country far from home. His courage reminds us that the values of duty, sacrifice, and service know no borders.

lessons.reflection: Captain Salaria fought thousands of kilometres away from India while serving under the United Nations flag. He willingly risked—and ultimately gave—his life to protect peace in another country. What does his story teach us about serving humanity beyond national borders? Can helping people we have never met be one of the highest forms of service?

quiz.question: Which regiment did Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria serve with during the United Nations mission in the Congo?
Which United Nations peacekeeping mission was Captain Salaria serving in when he was awarded the Param Vir Chakra?
options: United Nations Operation in the Congo (ONUC)
United Nations Mission in Sierra Leone (UNAMSIL)
United Nations Mission in South Sudan (UNMISS)

quiz.question: Why is Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria's Param Vir Chakra historically unique?
options: He was the youngest Param Vir Chakra recipient.
He was the first Gorkha officer to receive the award.
He is the only Indian to receive the Param Vir Chakra while serving in a United Nations peacekeeping mission.
He received the award during the Second World War.

quiz.question: How did Captain Salaria begin his assault against the Katangese Gendarmerie?
options: By destroying two enemy armoured vehicles with a rocket launcher before leading a close-quarters assault
By laying anti-tank mines across the road

quiz.question: How old was Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria when he made the supreme sacrifice in the Congo?

lessons.rank: Honorary Captain (Naib Subedar at the time of the action)
lessons.value: Captured the seemingly impregnable Quaid Post at over 21,000 feet during one of the most daring high-altitude assaults in military history.
lessons.hook: At over 21,000 feet on the world's highest battlefield, Naib Subedar Bana Singh led a small assault team up a near-vertical ice wall to capture a heavily defended enemy post that many believed was impossible to reach.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Bana Singh was born on 3 January 1949 in Kadyal village, Jammu district (now Samba district), Jammu & Kashmir. He joined the Indian Army and served with the 8 Jammu & Kashmir Light Infantry, earning a reputation for courage, discipline, and exceptional physical endurance. By 1987, he was serving as a Naib Subedar on the Siachen Glacier, where Indian and Pakistani troops were engaged in one of the harshest military deployments in the world.

Since Operation Meghdoot in 1984, India had occupied key positions on the Siachen Glacier. In early 1987, Pakistani forces established a fortified position known as Quaid Post on a towering ice feature overlooking Indian supply routes. Situated at an altitude of more than 21,000 feet, the post dominated the surrounding area and was protected by sheer ice cliffs, extreme weather, and heavy defensive fire, making it appear almost impossible to capture.

On 23 June 1987, during Operation Rajiv, Naib Subedar Bana Singh volunteered to lead a small assault team to capture the post. Using a route the enemy considered impossible, his team climbed a near-vertical ice wall through blizzard conditions to approach the bunker from an unexpected direction. Reaching the summit, they launched a surprise close-quarters assault using rifles, grenades, bayonets, and hand-to-hand combat. Despite the extreme altitude, freezing temperatures, and enemy resistance, the team successfully captured the post. For his exceptional courage, inspiring leadership, and determination under impossible conditions, Bana Singh was awarded the Param Vir Chakra. The captured position was later renamed Bana Post in his honour.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body:
The capture of Quaid Post transformed the tactical situation on the Siachen Glacier. By removing a dominating Pakistani position, Indian forces secured greater control over a critical sector of the glacier and strengthened their defensive posture. The operation demonstrated that careful planning, determination, and extraordinary leadership could overcome even the most difficult geographical and military challenges.

Bana Singh's success became one of the defining moments in the history of the Indian Army. It reinforced India's resolve to defend Siachen despite its extreme climate, where avalanches, altitude sickness, and severe cold often pose greater dangers than enemy fire.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body:
Honorary Captain Bana Singh remains one of India's most respected living war heroes and one of the few living recipients of the Param Vir Chakra. The captured Bana Post continues to bear his name, serving as a lasting reminder of one of the most remarkable high-altitude assaults ever undertaken.

His achievements are taught in military institutions as an outstanding example of leadership, planning, teamwork, and courage under extreme conditions. In recognition of his lifelong service to the nation, he was also awarded the Padma Bhushan. His story inspires generations of Indians to believe that determination and teamwork can overcome even the greatest obstacles.

lessons.reflection: At an altitude where simply breathing is difficult, Bana Singh and his team climbed an almost impossible route to accomplish their mission. Their success was built not only on courage, but also on preparation, teamwork, and trust in one another. Why do you think teamwork becomes even more important when facing extremely difficult challenges? How can we apply the same spirit in our everyday lives?

quiz.question: Which regiment did Naib Subedar Bana Singh serve with during Operation Rajiv in 1987?
What was the original name of the Pakistani post captured by Bana Singh's team?
Approximately how high above sea level was Quaid Post located?
Which military operation led to the capture of Quaid Post?
What honour was given to the captured post after the successful assault?
options: It retained the name Quaid Post.

lessons.value: Turned a deadly LTTE ambush into a successful counterattack through exceptional leadership and courage.
lessons.hook: Caught in a deadly ambush in Sri Lanka, Major Ramaswamy Parameswaran refused to retreat. Even after being shot at close range, he continued leading his soldiers until victory was secured.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Major Ramaswamy Parameswaran was born on 13 September 1946 in Bombay (now Mumbai), Maharashtra. He was commissioned into the Mahar Regiment in 1972 and quickly earned a reputation as a disciplined, compassionate, and highly professional officer. Well known for his cheerful personality, physical fitness, and concern for the welfare of his soldiers, he inspired confidence wherever he served.

In 1987, India deployed the Indian Peace Keeping Force (IPKF) to Sri Lanka under the Indo–Sri Lanka Accord. Initially tasked with maintaining peace and helping disarm militant groups, the mission soon turned into intense counter-insurgency operations against the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE). The challenging terrain, unfamiliar environment, and guerrilla tactics made the operation one of the Indian Army's most demanding overseas deployments.

On the night of 25 November 1987, while returning from a search operation near Uduvil on the Jaffna Peninsula, Major Parameswaran's company was suddenly ambushed by LTTE militants. Reacting instantly, he surrounded the attackers from the rear and led a bold counterattack instead of remaining pinned down. During the fierce close-quarters fighting, an LTTE militant shot him at point-blank range. Despite being seriously wounded, Major Parameswaran grabbed the attacker, wrested away his rifle, killed him, and continued directing his men. Inspired by his fearless leadership, the company defeated the ambush, recovering several enemy weapons and inflicting heavy losses on the militants. Major Parameswaran later succumbed to his injuries. For his extraordinary courage, leadership, and supreme sacrifice, he was posthumously awarded the Param Vir Chakra.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body:
Operation Pawan remains one of the most complex military operations undertaken by independent India. Fighting a well-organised insurgent force in unfamiliar territory demanded exceptional leadership and adaptability from Indian soldiers. Major Parameswaran's actions demonstrated the importance of initiative, courage, and decisive leadership under the most difficult circumstances.

His bravery became a symbol of the sacrifices made by thousands of Indian soldiers who served with the Indian Peace Keeping Force in Sri Lanka. His Param Vir Chakra honours not only his personal heroism but also the dedication of all those who served during this challenging mission.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body:
Major Ramaswamy Parameswaran is remembered as one of India's finest military leaders and among the few Param Vir Chakra recipients honoured for actions during overseas operations. His story is studied in military institutions as an outstanding example of leadership under fire and the importance of taking decisive action during an ambush.

His name is commemorated at the National War Memorial and by the Mahar Regiment, while roads and institutions have also been named in his honour. His life reminds us that true courage is not the absence of fear but the determination to lead others despite danger, uncertainty, and personal sacrifice.

lessons.reflection: When faced with a sudden ambush, Major Parameswaran chose to take the initiative instead of waiting for events to unfold. Even after being seriously wounded, he continued to lead his soldiers until the mission was accomplished. What does his story teach us about staying calm during unexpected challenges? How can taking positive action instead of giving up help us overcome difficulties in everyday life?

quiz.question: Which regiment did Major Ramaswamy Parameswaran serve with?

options: He grabbed the attacker, killed him with his own rifle, and continued leading his company.
He surrendered to protect his soldiers.

lessons.regiment: 7 Bihar Regiment; National Security Guard (51 Special Action Group)
lessons.war: Operation Black Tornado – 2008 Mumbai Terrorist Attacks
lessons.value: Led NSG commandos into the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel and made the supreme sacrifice while protecting hostages and his fellow commandos.
lessons.hook: When terrorists seized Mumbai's iconic Taj Hotel, Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan led his NSG commandos into the burning building, placing the lives of civilians and his teammates above his own.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan was born on 15 March 1977 in Kozhikode, Kerala, and grew up in Bengaluru, Karnataka. Inspired by a desire to serve the nation, he joined the National Defence Academy in Pune and was commissioned into the 7 Bihar Regiment in 1999. Early in his career, he participated in Operation Vijay during the Kargil War, gaining valuable operational experience that shaped him into an outstanding infantry officer.

Recognised for his exceptional leadership, physical endurance, and tactical ability, he was selected for the National Security Guard (NSG), India's elite counter-terrorism force. As an officer of the 51 Special Action Group, he underwent rigorous training in hostage rescue, close-quarters combat, and urban counter-terrorism operations, preparing for missions where every second could decide the fate of innocent lives.

On 26 November 2008, ten Lashkar-e-Taiba terrorists launched coordinated attacks across Mumbai, targeting multiple public locations, including the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel. Major Unnikrishnan led an NSG assault team tasked with rescuing hostages and eliminating the terrorists inside the hotel. Moving through smoke-filled corridors under constant threat, he led from the front while clearing rooms and guiding civilians to safety.

During the operation, one of his commandos was seriously wounded. Major Unnikrishnan ensured his injured colleague could be evacuated before continuing the pursuit of the terrorists. While pressing the assault on the upper floors, he was fatally wounded. His courage, leadership, and selfless commitment to the mission inspired his team to continue the operation until the hotel was fully secured. For his extraordinary bravery and supreme sacrifice, he was posthumously awarded the Ashok Chakra, India's highest peacetime gallantry award.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body:
The 26/11 Mumbai attacks transformed India's approach to counter-terrorism and national security. Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan became the face of the courage displayed by the National Security Guard during one of the country's most challenging hostage-rescue operations. His actions demonstrated the extraordinary risks undertaken by special forces personnel who enter dangerous situations to protect innocent civilians.

His sacrifice inspired widespread respect for India's armed forces and reinforced the importance of professionalism, teamwork, and selfless leadership in counter-terrorism operations. His Ashok Chakra recognises not only his personal heroism but also the dedication of every soldier who willingly places others' safety before their own.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body:
Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan remains one of India's most admired military heroes. His courage is commemorated at the National War Memorial, the National Security Guard headquarters, and through memorials, schools, and roads named in his honour across India. His life has also been portrayed in books, documentaries, and films, inspiring millions of young Indians.

His famous words, 'Do not come up, I will handle them,' have become a symbol of fearless leadership and complete devotion to duty. They reflect a leader who accepted the greatest risk himself so that others could live.

Major Unnikrishnan's story reminds us that heroism is not limited to the battlefield. Whether defending the nation's borders or protecting civilians from terrorism, courage, compassion, and leadership remain the defining qualities of a true soldier.

lessons.reflection: Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan repeatedly placed the safety of civilians and his fellow commandos above his own. His leadership was built on responsibility, courage, and selfless service. Why do great leaders willingly accept greater risks than those they lead? How can we demonstrate similar responsibility toward our family, friends, and community in everyday life?

quiz.question: Which regiment was Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan commissioned into after graduating from the National Defence Academy?
Which elite force was Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan serving with during the 2008 Mumbai terrorist attacks?
options: National Security Guard (51 Special Action Group)

options: He stayed at the command centre directing operations.

quiz.question: Which gallantry award was Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan posthumously awarded for his actions during the Mumbai terrorist attacks?

modules.title: Beyond the Course — Legends Worth Knowing
modules.war: Multiple Conflicts: 1947–48, 1971, and the Making of the Indian Army
modules.focus: Five extraordinary figures whose stories span the full arc of independent India's military life — a Field Marshal who built the army from nothing, a Jemadar who charged a defended ridge four times, a Brigadier who chose India over Pakistan at Partition, a naval captain who went down with his ship, and a major who held a desert post against 65 tanks.
modules.activity: Choose one hero from this module and write a one-page tribute in any format — a letter, a poem, a newspaper editorial, or a speech. Do not summarise their story; focus on the one lesson from their life that speaks most directly to you, and explain why.
modules.challenge: Visit the National War Memorial in New Delhi, online or in person. Find the names of at least two heroes from this course inscribed among the 26,000 names on its walls. Reflect on what it means to be remembered this way — a name on stone, instead of a life in time.
modules.summary:
This optional bonus module gathers five stories that deserve to stand alongside the heroes of the main course — stories that span the entire arc of independent India's military history, from the Jemadar who silenced three machine-gun posts alone on a Burmese ridge in 1944, to the naval captain who stayed on the bridge of his sinking ship in the Arabian Sea in 1971.

They represent different services, different eras, and profoundly different kinds of courage. Field Marshal K.M. Cariappa built the Indian Army from the ground up, becoming its first Indian Commander-in-Chief and establishing the institutional standards that would guide it through the conflicts that followed. Brigadier Mohammad Usman made a choice at Partition that defined who he was — and then gave his life defending that choice at Nowshera. Jemadar Nand Singh of 1/11 Sikh Regiment performed a feat of individual ferocity in the Burma Campaign of World War II — charging a defended ridge four times, wounded and bleeding, until the position was his. Captain Mahendra Nath Mulla of the Indian Navy became the first naval recipient of the Maha Vir Chakra when he stayed on the bridge of INS Khukri as it sank, ensuring as many of his crew as possible could escape. And Major Kuldip Singh Chandpuri held the Longewala post with 120 men against 65 tanks and 2,000 soldiers through a desert night — refusing, simply, to withdraw.

These are not supplementary stories. They are essential ones. Together they form a portrait of a military tradition that, across every service and every era, has produced soldiers of extraordinary moral and physical courage — and of the quiet, deliberate choices that separate duty from survival.

lessons.regiment: Rajput Regiment; later Indian Army Chief
lessons.award: Order of the Chief Commander, Legion of Merit (USA); Field Marshal (1986)
lessons.value: First Indian Commander-in-Chief of the Indian Army — the man who built the institution
lessons.hook: When India became free, it inherited an army still largely commanded by British officers. One man was given the task of making it truly Indian — and he shaped everything that followed.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Kodandera Madappa Cariappa was born on 28 January 1899 in Coorg (today Kodagu), Karnataka, into a family of the Kodava community — a people with a martial tradition stretching back centuries, known for producing soldiers of exceptional discipline and resolve. He was among the first Indians selected for the batch of King's Commissioned Indian Officers at Daly Cadet College, Indore, receiving his permanent commission in 1922 and being transferred to Mesopotamia (present-day Iraq). On his return to India he was posted to the Rajput Regiment, which became his primary regimental home through much of his career.

Career milestone followed career milestone: in 1933, Cariappa became the first Indian officer to complete the prestigious Staff College course at Quetta — an achievement that marked him as a serious military professional in an era when such distinctions were rare for Indian officers. In 1942, during World War II, he became the first Indian to be given command of an infantry unit — a watershed in an army still largely commanded by British officers. He saw active service in Iraq, Syria, Iran, and Burma. In 1947, he became the first Indian officer selected for the Imperial Defence College course at Camberley, UK — the most senior professional development appointment in the British military world.

At Partition in August 1947, Cariappa was given the delicate and immensely important task of overseeing the division of the British Indian Army's assets and personnel between India and Pakistan — a role demanding administrative precision, political sensitivity, and personal authority. When the 1947–48 Kashmir War broke out, he served as Eastern Army Commander and then General Officer Commanding-in-Chief of Western Command, personally leading operations that recaptured Zojila, Drass, and Kargil — some of the highest-altitude military operations in the history of modern warfare.

On 15 January 1949, General Cariappa became the first Indian Commander-in-Chief of the Indian Army, taking over from General Sir Francis Butcher. He retired in 1953 and went on to serve as India's High Commissioner to Australia and New Zealand. The Government of India conferred the five-star rank of Field Marshal on Cariappa in 1986, one of only two Indians ever to hold that rank. He died on 15 May 1993 at the age of 94, having outlived most of the generation he led, and having watched the army he built fight five wars and endure into the new century.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: K.M. Cariappa's impact on the Indian Army is foundational in the most literal sense. He inherited an army whose officer corps was transitioning from British to Indian command — with all the institutional uncertainty, professional adjustment, and political sensitivity that entailed — and he set the standards, the culture, and the professional ethos that the army carried forward into every conflict that followed. His insistence on discipline, political neutrality, and meritocracy shaped the officer culture that produced the leaders of 1962, 1965, and 1971. American President Harry S. Truman awarded him the Order of the Chief Commander of the Legion of Merit in recognition of his contribution to Allied cooperation. That 15 January 1949 is now celebrated annually as Army Day in India is the most fitting of all tributes: a whole nation marking the day one man took command of its army and made it his own.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Field Marshal Cariappa's name is perpetuated across India's military institutions — the Cariappa College at the Indian Military Academy, training grounds, and military schools all carry his name. He is one of only two Indians ever to hold the five-star rank of Field Marshal, the other being Sam Manekshaw. A man of immense personal discipline and dignity, he was known for his insistence on high standards, his principled opposition to communalism within the army, and his genuine accessibility to soldiers of every rank. There is a story about him that, more than anything else, defines his character: his own son, Flight Lieutenant K.C. 'Nanda' Cariappa, was shot down and captured by Pakistan during the 1965 war. When Pakistan offered to release Nanda as a gesture of goodwill toward his famous father, Field Marshal Cariappa wrote to President Ayub Khan refusing the offer — asking simply that his son be treated no differently from any other Indian prisoner of war. That letter may be the finest summary of who Cariappa was: a man to whom the institution, and the soldiers in it, were always more important than any personal exemption.

lessons.reflection: When Cariappa's son was captured as a prisoner of war, Pakistan offered a personal favour — release him out of respect for his father. Cariappa refused. What does this tell us about the relationship between personal love and public duty? Is it possible to hold both at once — and what does choosing public duty over personal benefit cost the person who does it?

lessons.rank: Jemadar (then Naik at time of action)
lessons.award: Victoria Cross (World War II); Indian Order of Merit
lessons.value: Charged a steep ridge alone four times, wiping out three machine-gun posts — one of the supreme individual acts of gallantry in Indian military history
lessons.hook: The ridge was held by the Japanese, machine guns covering every approach from above. The leading section was pinned down. Nand Singh charged it alone — and he charged it four times.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Nand Singh was born in 1914 in Shahpur district of Punjab (in present-day Pakistan) into a Sikh family with a long tradition of soldiering. He enlisted in the 1/11 Sikh Regiment of the British Indian Army and by 1944 had served through several theatres, rising to the rank of Naik — a junior NCO. By March 1944, his battalion was in Burma — the most demanding theatre of World War II for Indian troops, a war of dense jungle, mountainous terrain, tropical disease, and an experienced, ferocious Japanese enemy who had already overrun Singapore and swept through South-East Asia.

On 11–12 March 1944, near Maungdaw in the Arakan region of Burma, Nand Singh's platoon was ordered to capture a strongly held Japanese position at the top of a steep ridge. The position was fortified with well-constructed bunkers and covered by multiple machine-gun posts that swept every approach with fire. The leading section of the platoon was pinned down almost immediately, unable to advance or withdraw without taking catastrophic casualties.

Nand Singh did not wait for orders. He charged the ridge alone. He was wounded reaching the top — and destroyed the first machine-gun post with grenades and his bayonet. He was wounded again. He charged again and wiped out the second post. Wounded a third time, bleeding from multiple wounds, he charged again and destroyed a third machine-gun post. By the time the position was cleared, Nand Singh had personally accounted for the entire defensive line that had stopped his platoon. The ridge was taken.

Nand Singh was awarded the Victoria Cross — the highest gallantry decoration in the British Empire — for this action. He was one of the last Indian soldiers to receive the VC before Independence, and one of the most decorated non-commissioned officers in the history of the subcontinent.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The battle at Maungdaw was part of the broader Arakan campaign that eventually helped liberate Burma from Japanese occupation — a campaign in which Indian soldiers played by far the largest role, accounting for the majority of Allied troops in the Southeast Asian theatre. The contribution of the Indian Army to the Burma Campaign is one of the most significant and least celebrated chapters of World War II military history. Nand Singh's feat is remarkable even within the extraordinary annals of that war: charging a defended ridge four times, sustaining wounds on three separate charges, and personally destroying three machine-gun posts is an act of individual aggression and physical stamina that few soldiers in any army in any war have matched. His Victoria Cross was the recognition — by the highest authority then available — that what he did was in a class by itself.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Jemadar Nand Singh's story is less widely known to general Indian audiences than those of the Param Vir Chakra recipients, partly because his defining action took place in World War II — before India's independence — and partly because the Burma Campaign has been underrepresented in popular Indian memory relative to its scale and significance. Within the 1/11 Sikh Regiment, however, he is honoured as one of the regiment's greatest soldiers. His Victoria Cross is a permanent reminder that Indian soldiers fought — and fought magnificently — in every major theatre of World War II, long before India had its own gallantry award system. He lived until 1986, which means the Indian Army had a living holder of the Victoria Cross for four decades after independence — a connection to that extraordinary generation of soldiers that most countries can only read about in history books.

lessons.reflection: Nand Singh was already wounded when he charged the ridge the second time. He was wounded again before the third charge. And still he went back. At what point does courage become something beyond what we normally understand by the word? What does a story like this tell us about the human capacity to override the most primal instinct — the one that says stop, you have done enough, stay alive?

Because the Victoria Cross was not considered equivalent to the Param Vir Chakra under Indian law

lessons.value: The Lion of Nowshera — chose India over a Pakistan Army commission
lessons.hook: At Partition, Pakistan offered him a general's commission on account of his religion. He said no — India was his country. He gave his life defending that answer.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Mohammad Usman was born on 18 July 1912 in Bibipur village in Mau district, United Provinces (present-day Uttar Pradesh), into a Muslim family. He was commissioned into the British Indian Army in 1934 from the Indian Military Academy at Dehradun, and by the time of Independence he had established a reputation as one of the finest infantry officers of his generation — disciplined, tactically astute, deeply respected by both his officers and his men across every boundary of rank, region, and religion. When Partition came in 1947 and the British Indian Army was divided between India and Pakistan, Usman faced a choice that would define the rest of his short life: he was offered a senior commission in the newly formed Pakistani Army, with immediate promotion, on account of his faith. He declined without hesitation. India was his country, he said plainly, and he would serve no other.

In early 1948, as Pakistani-backed tribal forces seized the town of Jhangar in the Jammu sector and then pressed toward Nowshera, Brigadier Usman was given command of the brigade tasked with holding and recovering these positions. When Nowshera came under sustained siege — completely surrounded, communications stretched, supplies dwindling — it was Usman's personal presence, his ability to inspire soldiers in circumstances that seemed hopeless, and his tactical judgement that held the garrison together. He organised a successful breakout of the siege, relieved the pressure on Nowshera, and then went on the offensive — personally leading the operations that recovered Jhangar. His soldiers called him 'The Lion of Nowshera,' a name that spread across the entire front.

On 3 July 1948, while inspecting forward positions near Naushera, Brigadier Usman was struck by an enemy artillery shell and killed instantly. He was 35 years old. He had reportedly told colleagues in his final months that he did not expect to survive the war — and did not need to. What mattered was that his men would know he stood with them to the end. He was given a state funeral and buried with full military honours at the Jamia Millia Islamia ground in Delhi, where tens of thousands came to pay their respects.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Mohammad Usman's impact on the 1947–48 war was both tactical and profoundly symbolic. Tactically, the defence and recovery of Nowshera and Jhangar denied Pakistani raiders a springboard from which they could have threatened Jammu itself — the winter capital of Jammu & Kashmir and the gateway to the plains of northern India. Had Nowshera fallen permanently, the consequences for India's entire position in the Jammu sector could have been severe and irreversible. Symbolically, his story — of a Muslim officer who chose India, fought for India, and died for India — was of enormous importance in the communally charged atmosphere of post-Partition India. He became one of the most powerful embodiments of the idea that the Indian Army, and India itself, was a nation defined not by religion or birth but by shared values, shared service, and deliberate choice. He was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra, India's second-highest wartime gallantry award.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Brigadier Mohammad Usman is remembered as one of the most important and most symbolically significant figures of the 1947–48 Kashmir War. His story is taught in Indian military academies as an example of both tactical excellence and moral clarity — rare to find together in the same man at the same moment. His grave at Jamia Millia Islamia ground in Delhi is maintained with great care, and soldiers and civilians come to pay their respects every year on the anniversary of his death. A road in Delhi bears his name. His decision to reject the Pakistani commission, made simply and without drama, resonates across generations as a statement about identity, loyalty, and what it means to be Indian — not as a matter of the accident of birth or religious affiliation, but as a matter of deliberate, courageous, irrevocable choice.

lessons.reflection: At Partition, the easiest path for Usman was to follow his religion into Pakistan — a senior commission, immediate promotion, a country shaped around his faith. He chose India instead, and gave his life for that choice. What does his decision tell us about the difference between loyalty to a religious identity and loyalty to a national one? And what does it mean, in the deepest sense, to choose your country?

lessons.value: First Indian Navy recipient of the Maha Vir Chakra — went down with his ship ensuring his crew could escape
lessons.hook: Two torpedoes struck INS Khukri. The ship had three minutes. His crew was abandoning vessel — and Captain Mulla stayed on the bridge until the sea came up to meet him.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Mahendra Nath Mulla was born on 15 May 1926 in Gorakhpur, Uttar Pradesh. He was commissioned into the Indian Navy on 1 May 1948 — just months after Indian independence — and over the next two decades rose steadily through the service, accumulating the experience and professional standing that brought him, by December 1971, to the command of INS Khukri. The Khukri was a Type 14 anti-submarine frigate of the Western Fleet — a versatile, capable ship, one of the workhorses of the Navy's operational capability.

The 1971 war placed the Indian Navy in active combat for the first time since independence. On the Western seaboard, Pakistan's most dangerous naval asset was PNS Hangor — a French-built Daphné-class submarine commanded by Commander Ahmad Tasnim, which had been quietly operating in the waters off India's western coast. On the evening of 9 December 1971, INS Khukri and its sister ship INS Kirpan were conducting anti-submarine operations in the Arabian Sea off the coast of Diu. The Hangor evaded their search. At approximately 20:57, PNS Hangor fired two torpedoes at INS Khukri. Both struck. The resulting explosions were catastrophic — INS Khukri sank in approximately three minutes, taking 18 officers and 176 sailors down with her.

In those three minutes, Captain Mulla displayed the qualities that define a commanding officer of the highest order. He remained on the bridge, calm and composed, directing the emergency, organising the abandonment of the vessel, and ensuring that as many of his crew as possible could get clear of the sinking ship. He did not abandon his vessel. He went down with her. Of the ship's company, 67 officers and men survived. Captain Mulla's conduct — his refusal to place his own survival above his duty to his men and his ship — was recognised by the posthumous award of the Maha Vir Chakra, making him the first officer of the Indian Navy to receive it.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The sinking of INS Khukri was the most significant naval combat loss India has suffered since independence. In the context of the 1971 war it was a tactical Pakistani success — PNS Hangor had demonstrated that a well-handled submarine could penetrate Indian anti-submarine defences and operate effectively in contested waters. INS Khukri's loss removed a capable surface asset from the Western Fleet at a critical moment. Yet the manner in which Captain Mulla commanded his ship in its final three minutes — calm, composed, focused on his men rather than his own survival — became an enduring standard within the Indian Navy for what is expected of a commanding officer when everything is lost. His legacy transformed a moment of devastation into one of the most lasting examples of naval leadership in India's military history.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Captain Mahendra Nath Mulla is honoured in the Indian Navy as the definitive embodiment of the commanding officer's ultimate responsibility: a ship's captain stays with his vessel. His Maha Vir Chakra is the first such award in the Navy's history, and every year on 9 December — the anniversary of the sinking — the Indian Navy observes a memorial for INS Khukri and those who were lost with her. A permanent memorial to the ship and her crew stands at Diu, near the site of the sinking. Captain Mulla's composure in the final three minutes of his life — with the ship going down around him, his crew in the water — continues to inspire generations of Indian naval officers as the definitive example of what command means at its most extreme: not rank, not ceremony, but the quiet insistence on doing your duty while everything else is being taken away.

lessons.reflection: Captain Mulla had approximately three minutes from the first torpedo strike to the ship going under. He chose to stay on the bridge. What does it mean to hold to a standard of duty when every instinct, every rational calculation, says: go now. Can you think of a moment — in any context, at any scale — where you held to something difficult because it was right, even when no one would have blamed you for letting it go?

lessons.rank: Major (at time of battle); later promoted to Brigadier
lessons.value: Held Longewala — 120 men against 2,000 soldiers and 65 tanks
lessons.hook: His post had 120 men, one anti-tank gun, and no reinforcements coming. The enemy had 65 tanks and 2,000 soldiers. He chose to hold.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Kuldip Singh Chandpuri was born on 22 November 1940 in Lyallpur — now Faisalabad, Pakistan — and came to India as a young child at Partition. Commissioned into the Indian Army, he rose to command a company of the 23 Punjab Regiment by 1971. He was a major when the war began.

On the night of 4–5 December 1971, a massive Pakistani armoured column crossed the border into the Thar Desert of Rajasthan and advanced on the small border post at Longewala. The column comprised approximately 65 tanks — mostly US-supplied M47 and M48 Pattons — and around 2,000 infantry soldiers, with a combined arms force designed to punch deep into Rajasthan and threaten Jaisalmer. The Longewala post had one company of 23 Punjab: about 120 men and a single American-made 106 mm recoilless rifle used as an anti-tank gun.

When the advancing column was detected, Chandpuri had two options: request air support and hold until dawn, or withdraw his company to avoid annihilation. His commanding officer told him he could withdraw if he judged the position untenable. Chandpuri refused to withdraw. He spread his men around the perimeter, ordered his one anti-tank gun into position, and called for IAF support. Through the long desert night, his company held the post — the recoilless rifle destroying tank after tank that tried to breach the berm, while Chandpuri kept his men calm and in position.

At dawn, the Indian Air Force arrived. HAL HF-24 Marut and Hawker Hunter jets of the Western Air Command descended on the Pakistani column, which was now stalled, exposed in open desert, and unable to manoeuvre. Thirty-six Pakistani tanks were destroyed. Over 200 Pakistani soldiers were killed. The column retreated in disorder. India's losses at Longewala: two soldiers. The battle was over.

Chandpuri was awarded the Maha Vir Chakra. He later rose to the rank of Brigadier before retiring. He died on 17 November 2018, just days before what would have been his 78th birthday.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The Battle of Longewala had strategic importance beyond its modest scale. Pakistan's plan in the Rajasthan sector was to use armoured columns to penetrate deep into Indian territory — to create facts on the ground that could offset the disaster unfolding in the east. Had the column broken through Longewala, it would have advanced on Jaisalmer and destabilised India's position on the Western front at a crucial moment. Chandpuri's decision to hold, and the IAF's ability to respond at dawn, turned what was designed to be a breakthrough into a rout. The battle became the defining symbol of the 1971 war on the Western front: that small, determined forces, properly supported and properly led, can defeat overwhelmingly superior enemies.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: The Battle of Longewala was immortalised in J.P. Dutta's 1997 Bollywood epic 'Border', starring Sunny Deol as Major Chandpuri, Jackie Shroff, and Akshaye Khanna. It remains one of the highest-grossing war films in Indian cinema history and has introduced the story of Longewala to multiple generations. A war memorial now stands at Longewala, where some of the destroyed Pakistani tanks remain on display — a permanent, rusting reminder of the night 120 men held the desert. Brigadier Chandpuri himself attended many commemorations and screenings over the years, always insisting that the real heroes were his men, not him. He retired a modest, quietly proud soldier, and died in 2018 knowing the country remembered.

lessons.reflection: Chandpuri held Longewala with 120 men against 2,000 soldiers and 65 tanks — and chose not to withdraw when he was given the option. What is the difference between stubbornness and steadfast resolve? How do you know, in the moment, which one you are exercising?
